title: Parker, Arizona

infobox.value: Jan | Feb | Mar | Apr | May | Jun | Jul | Aug | Sep | Oct | Nov | Dec | Year

([Mojave](/wiki/Mojave_language "Mojave language") '**Amat Kuhwely**, formerly '**Ahwe Nyava**) is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [La Paz County](/wiki/La_Paz_County,_Arizona "La Paz County, Arizona"), [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), United States,[[4]](#cite_note-GR6-4) on the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") in [Parker Valley](/wiki/Parker_Valley "Parker Valley"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population of the city was 3,417.

**Parker** ([Mojave](/wiki/Mojave_language "Mojave language") '**Amat Kuhwely**, formerly '**Ahwe Nyava**) is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [La Paz County](/wiki/La_Paz_County,_Arizona "La Paz County, Arizona"), [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), United States,[[4]](#cite_note-GR6-4) on the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") in [Parker Valley](/wiki/Parker_Valley "Parker Valley"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population of the city was 3,417.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parker,_Arizona&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Founded in 1908, the town was named after [Ely Parker](/wiki/Ely_Parker "Ely Parker"), the first Native American commissioner for the U.S. government. The original town site of Parker was surveyed and laid out in 1909 by Earl H. Parker, a railroad location engineer for the [Arizona & California Railway](/wiki/Arizona_and_California_Railroad#Arizona_and_California_Railway "Arizona and California Railroad"). The town officially incorporated in 1948 and became the county seat for the newly created La Paz County on January 1, 1983.

### Camp Colorado and Parkers Landing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parker,_Arizona&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Camp Colorado and Parkers Landing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Arizona_-_Parker_-_NARA_-_23933749.jpg/250px-Arizona_-_Parker_-_NARA_-_23933749.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arizona_-_Parker_-_NARA_-_23933749.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Arizona_-_Parker_-_NARA_-_23933747.jpg/250px-Arizona_-_Parker_-_NARA_-_23933747.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arizona_-_Parker_-_NARA_-_23933747.jpg)

The town's name and origin began when a post office called **Parker** was established January 6, 1871, at [Parker's Landing](/w/index.php?title=Parker%27s_Landing,_Arizona&action=edit&redlink=1 "Parker's Landing, Arizona (page does not exist)") and the site of the Parker Indian Agency, named for Ely Parker, on the [Colorado River Indian Reservation](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Reservation "Colorado River Indian Reservation"), four miles downriver from the site of the railroad bridge of the modern town, to serve the Indian agency.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Theobald-7): 118 What became Parkers Landing was established on the river as the place to land and pick up cargo and personnel for the Indian Agency and the [U. S. Army](/wiki/U._S._Army "U. S. Army") detachment that was stationed there at [Camp Colorado](/w/index.php?title=Camp_Colorado,_Arizona&action=edit&redlink=1 "Camp Colorado, Arizona (page does not exist)") from 1864 to 1869, during the first years of the Reservation. Camp Colorado was abandoned after sparks from the departing steamboat [Cocopah](/wiki/Cocopah_II_(Sternwheeler) "Cocopah II (Sternwheeler)") rapidly burned down the brush huts of the officers of the garrison, and endangered its barracks and storehouses.
[[8]](#cite_note-Lingenfelter-8) : 66, n.61[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parker,_Arizona&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

The city is on the [Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River "Colorado River") just south of the Headgate Rock Dam and Moovalya Lake. [Arizona State Route 95](/wiki/Arizona_State_Route_95 "Arizona State Route 95") and [California State Route 62](/wiki/California_State_Route_62 "California State Route 62") (across the Colorado River) serve the city.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the town has a total area of 22.0 square miles (57 km2), of which 22.0 square miles (57 km2) is land and 0.05% is water.

According to Census Bureau maps, the town is divided into two non-contiguous sections; the northern section consists of the original town and is located in the [Colorado River Indian reservation](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Tribes "Colorado River Indian Tribes") and the southern section consists of a larger, roughly rectangular section of largely undeveloped territory. The undeveloped territory consist of extensive unpaved roads. Of the few that are paved, Mohave Road is the most utilized between the town and the territory. The same road can be taken 15.5 miles south to the town of [Poston](/wiki/Poston,_Arizona "Poston, Arizona"), which is noted for its relocation camps for [Japanese Americans](/wiki/Japanese_Americans "Japanese Americans") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parker,_Arizona&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

Parker has an [arid climate](/wiki/Arid_climate "Arid climate") classification, which is characterized by extremely hot summers and warm winters.

Wintertime highs in Parker are generally in the upper 60s to lower 70s. Lows during the winter are between 40 and 50 °F (4.4 and 10.0 °C) with an occasional morning dipping below 32 °F (0 °C). The all-time lowest recorded temperature in Parker occurred on December 31, 1911, when temperatures bottomed out at 9 °F (−12.8 °C); however, snow has been recorded only once when 3.0 inches (0.08 m) fell on one day in December 1932,[[11]](#cite_note-Precipitation-11) and no maximum below 32 °F (0 °C) has ever been observed.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Summers in Parker can be dangerously hot, with highs in June, July, August, and September remaining in the 100 to 110 °F (37.8 to 43.3 °C) range, days over 115 °F (46.1 °C) or even 120 °F (48.9 °C) are not rare.

April and May both average 90 to 100 °F (32.2 to 37.8 °C) daytime highs. Even the month of October has an average high of 90.0 °F (32.2 °C). The all-time highest recorded temperature in Parker was 127 °F (52.8 °C), which occurred on July 7, 1905. This was, at the time, the all-time record high temperature in Arizona history until [Lake Havasu City](/wiki/Lake_Havasu_City "Lake Havasu City") reached 128 °F (53.3 °C) on June 29, 1994.

Rainfall is uniformly scanty throughout the year in Parker, with no month averaging more than three days with measurable rainfall. The [monsoon](/wiki/North_American_Monsoon "North American Monsoon") storms that provide much of the rain in southern and eastern parts of Arizona seldom effect Parker. The wettest month since records began in 1893 was September 1939, when a rare decaying [hurricane](/wiki/Hurricane "Hurricane") produced a total of 8.85 inches (224.8 mm) including a record daily total of 3.41 inches (86.6 mm) on the fifth day of that month. The wettest calendar year has been 1992 with 13.59 inches (345.2 mm) – though from July 1992 to June 1993 the figure was slightly higher still at 13.74 inches (349.0 mm) – and the driest 1956 with 0.34 inches (8.6 mm).[[11]](#cite_note-Precipitation-11)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parker,_Arizona&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[19]](#cite_note-GR2-19) in 2000, there were 3,140 people, 1,064 households, and 791 families residing in the town. The population density was 142.8 inhabitants per square mile (55.1/km2). There were 1,157 housing units at an average density of 52.6 per square mile (20.3/km2). The racial makeup of the town was 62.0% [White](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 1.9% [Black](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 23.1% [Native American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.9% [Asian](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.2% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 7.5% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 4.5% from two or more races. 29.8% of the population were [Hispanic](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") of any race.

There were 1,064 households, out of which 41.5% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 51.6% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 15.6% had a female householder with no husband present, and 25.6% were non-families. 20.6% of all households were made up of individuals, and 8.4% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.93 and the average family size was 3.38.

In the town, the population was spread out, with 32.8% under the age of 18, 9.6% from 18 to 24, 25.4% from 25 to 44, 22.8% from 45 to 64, and 9.4% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 32 years. For every 100 females, there were 93.9 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 88.4 males.

The median income for a household in the town was $34,625, and the median income for a family was $37,663. Males had a median income of $26,542 versus $21,006 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the town was $15,016. About 10.6% of families and 14.7% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 18.2% of those under age 18 and 13.9% of those age 65 or over.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Parker,_Arizona&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[Arizona State Route 95](/wiki/Arizona_State_Route_95 "Arizona State Route 95") runs through Parker, entering the town from the northeast as Rio Vista Highway then turning south in the intersection with California Avenue. To the north of the intersection with California Avenue is the State Route 95 truck spur, which leads to the Colorado River bridge and the eastern terminus of [California State Route 62](/wiki/California_State_Route_62 "California State Route 62") in [Earp](/wiki/Earp,_California "Earp, California").

[Avi Suquilla Airport](/wiki/Avi_Suquilla_Airport "Avi Suquilla Airport") ([FAA](/wiki/Federal_Aviation_Administration "Federal Aviation Administration") [LID](/wiki/Location_identifier "Location identifier"): **P20**) is a public use [airport](/wiki/Airport "Airport") located one nautical mile (1.8 km) east of the [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district") of Parker. It is owned by the [Colorado River Indian Tribes](/wiki/Colorado_River_Indian_Tribes "Colorado River Indian Tribes").

The [Arizona & California Railroad](/wiki/Arizona_%26_California_Railroad "Arizona & California Railroad") is headquartered in Parker, after replacing the [Santa Fe Railway](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Railway "Santa Fe Railway") in 1991 as the owner and operator of the railroad line through the town. It crosses over the Colorado River on a five-span truss bridge near the road bridge to Earp.

La Paz County Health Department operates La Paz County Transit, an on-demand bus service.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
